%%
%% The abstract is a short summary of the work to be presented in the
%% article.
% \begin{abstract}
% % Retrieval augmentation can improve the robustness of frozen time-series foundation models (TSFMs) under distribution shift, but online retrieval adds latency and datastore overhead at inference time. We propose \textbf{TS-Memory}, a retrieval-to-memory framework that distills retrieval benefits into a plug-and-play memory module for strictly retrieval-free deployment. TS-Memory builds a leakage-safe offline teacher by retrieving similar contexts from the training split and summarizing their futures into confidence-aware quantile targets, and trains PlugMem via confidence-weighted distillation together with standard quantile supervision. PlugMem is backbone-agnostic and can be attached to any frozen TSFM without modifying backbone internals; a simple fusion rule combines memory and backbone forecasts with constant-time overhead independent of datastore size. Our contributions are: a search-to-memory adaptation paradigm for frozen TSFMs, a leakage-safe distributional teacher for retrieval-induced uncertainty, and a practical plug-and-play interface for retrieval-free serving. We conduct extensive experiments to validate TS-Memory's effectiveness. Our code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/TS-Memory-78D0/.

% Time Series Foundation Models (TSFMs) enable strong zero-shot forecasting via large-scale pretraining, yet adapting them to downstream domains remains challenging due to prevalent distribution shifts and domain heterogeneity. Existing strategies face a critical dilemma: \textit{Parametric Adaptation} risks catastrophic forgetting and high multi-domain maintenance costs, while \textit{Non-Parametric Retrieval} incurs prohibitive runtime latency from database searches. To address this, we propose \textit{Parametric Memory Distillation}—a framework distilling retrieval benefits into a lightweight learnable module—and instantiate it as \textbf{TS-Memory}, a backbone-agnostic, plug-and-play adapter for frozen TSFMs. Trained in two phases: an offline leakage-safe $k$NN teacher generates confidence-aware quantile targets from similar contexts, followed by distilling these distributional insights into the module via confidence-gated supervision. At inference, TS-Memory fuses memory and backbone predictions with constant-time overhead (datastore-size agnostic), enabling strictly retrieval-free serving. Extensive experiments across TSFMs and long-horizon benchmarks show TS-Memory consistently improves point/probabilistic forecasting accuracy—outperforming prior methods—while retaining near-baseline inference efficiency. Code: \url{https://anonymous.4open.science/r/TS-Memory-78D0/}.
% \end{abstract}


\begin{abstract}
% \textit{Time Series Foundation Models} (TSFMs) enable strong zero-shot forecasting via large-scale pre-training, yet adapting them to downstream domains remains challenging due to distribution shifts and domain heterogeneity. Existing strategies face a critical dilemma: \textit{Parametric Adaptation} risks catastrophic forgetting and high multi-domain maintenance costs, while \textit{Non-Parametric Retrieval} incurs prohibitive runtime latency from database searches. To bridge this gap, we propose \textit{Parametric Memory Distillation}---a framework distilling retrieval benefits into a lightweight learnable module---and instantiate it as \textbf{TS-Memory}, a backbone-agnostic, plug-and-play adapter for frozen TSFMs. Training proceeds in two phases: an offline leakage-safe $k$NN teacher generates confidence-aware quantile targets from similar contexts, and then distills these distributional insights into the module via confidence-gated supervision. At inference, TS-Memory fuses memory and backbone predictions with constant-time overhead (datastore-size agnostic), enabling strictly retrieval-free deployment. Extensive experiments across TSFMs and long-horizon benchmarks show that TS-Memory consistently improves point/probabilistic forecasting accuracy---outperforming prior methods---while retaining near-baseline inference efficiency. Code: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/TS-Memory-78D0/.


\textit{Time Series Foundation Models} (TSFMs) achieve strong zero-shot forecasting through large-scale pre-training, but adapting them to downstream domains under distribution shift remains challenging. Existing solutions face a trade-off: \textit{Parametric Adaptation} can cause catastrophic forgetting and requires costly multi-domain maintenance, while \textit{Non-Parametric Retrieval} improves forecasts but incurs high inference latency due to datastore search. We propose \textit{Parametric Memory Distillation} and implement it as \textbf{TS-Memory}, a lightweight memory adapter that augments frozen TSFMs. 
% TS-Memory is trained in two stages: we first build a leakage-safe $k$NN teacher offline to retrieve similar contexts and generate confidence-aware quantile targets, then train TS-Memory to learn these targets via confidence-gated supervision.
TS-Memory is trained in two stages. First, we construct an offline, retrieval-leakage-safe $k$NN teacher that synthesizes confidence-aware quantile targets from retrieved futures. Second, we distill this retrieval-induced distributional correction into a lightweight memory adapter via confidence-gated supervision.
During inference, TS-Memory fuses memory and backbone predictions with constant-time overhead, enabling retrieval-free deployment. Experiments across diverse TSFMs and benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements in both point and probabilistic forecasting over representative adaptation methods, with efficiency comparable to the frozen backbone. Code: https://github.com/sisuolv/TS-Memory.

\end{abstract}

}
